[Page 1]:
Group Relative Policy Optimization: Reducing PPO Variance for
Language Model Alignment

Authors: Z. Shao, D. Wang, Q. Zhu, R. Xu, K. Li, Y. Li, Y. Wu
DeepSeek-AI

Abstract

We present Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), a reinforcement
learning algorithm for aligning large language models that dispenses with
the value model used in PPO. Instead, GRPO samples a group of responses
per prompt and normalizes their rewards within the group to compute
advantages. This reduces memory consumption and stabilizes training on
large-scale models.

[Page 1]:
Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model

Rafael Rafailov, Archit Sharma, Eric Mitchell, Stefano Ermon, Christopher D. Manning,
Chelsea Finn

Stanford University

Abstract

While large-scale unsupervised language models (LMs) learn broad
world knowledge and some reasoning skills, achieving precise control
of their behavior is difficult due to the fully unsupervised nature
of their training. Existing methods for gaining such steerability
collect human labels of the relative quality of model generations
and fine-tune the unsupervised LM to align with these preferences,
often with reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF). In
this paper, we introduce a new parameterization of the reward model
in RLHF that enables extraction of the corresponding optimal policy
in closed form, allowing us to solve the standard RLHF problem
with only a simple classification loss.

[Page 1]:
LLaMA 3 Technical Report

Meta AI

Abstract

We introduce the LLaMA 3 herd of foundation models. LLaMA 3 is a
family of multilingual, multimodal foundation models trained on
15 trillion tokens with up to 405 billion parameters.

[Page 2]:
1. Introduction

Modern foundation models extend the reach of large language models
to more modalities — code, images, video, and speech — and to longer
contexts, with token windows now exceeding one million. In this
report, we detail the pre-training data curation, model architecture,
hyperparameter selection, and post-training methodology for LLaMA 3.

[Page 3]:
2. Pre-training

2.1. Data Composition
  - 50% general web (Common Crawl, filtered)
  - 25% code (GitHub, deduplicated at file level)
  - 17% mathematical reasoning (Proof-Pile, math stack exchange)
  - 8% multilingual (non-English web + Wikipedia)

Phase A1: Synthetic Training Data Pipeline Design

Godspeed Coding Agent — internal design doc
Author: Tremayne Timms
Date: 2026-04-17

1. Goal

Produce 6,200 tool-calling training samples in OpenAI {messages, tools}
format, using free-tier LLM providers only, at $0 budget.

2. Stratification

  - 70% single_tool (4,340 samples)
  - 15% multi_tool   (  930 samples)
  - 10% multi_turn   (  620 samples)
  -  5% no_tool      (  310 samples)

Floor: ≥295 samples per tool (21 tools × 295 = 6,195).

3. Pipeline

spec → GLM blueprint (Stage A) → real tool exec (Stage B) → GLM narration
(Stage C) → judge filter → validate → JSONL.

[Page 1]:
On the Measure of Intelligence

François Chollet
Google, Inc.

Abstract

To make deliberate progress towards more intelligent and more
human-like artificial systems, we need to be following an
appropriate feedback signal: we need to be able to define and
evaluate intelligence in a way that enables comparisons between
two systems, as well as comparisons with humans.

Over the past hundred years, there has been an abundance of
proposed formal definitions of intelligence in psychology and AI;
we document and contextualize the most influential among them.

[Page 1]:
DeepSeek-R1: Incentivizing Reasoning Capability in LLMs via
Reinforcement Learning

DeepSeek-AI

Abstract

We introduce DeepSeek-R1, a reasoning model trained via large-scale
reinforcement learning without supervised fine-tuning as a preliminary
step. DeepSeek-R1-Zero demonstrates impressive reasoning behaviors
emerging from pure RL with a rule-based reward. DeepSeek-R1
additionally incorporates multi-stage training and cold-start data
before RL, and achieves parity with o1-1217 on reasoning tasks.

[Page 1]:
Attention Is All You Need

Ashish Vaswani, Noam Shazeer, Niki Parmar, Jakob Uszkoreit, Llion Jones,
Aidan N. Gomez, Łukasz Kaiser, Illia Polosukhin

Google Brain / Google Research / University of Toronto

Abstract

The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex
recurrent or convolutional neural networks that include an encoder
and a decoder. The best performing models also connect the encoder
and decoder through an attention mechanism. We propose a new simple
network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention
mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely.

[Page 1]:
Incident postmortem: auth-service refresh token rejection spike

Date: 2026-04-09
Severity: SEV-3
Authors: sre-oncall, ttimms

## Summary

For 73 minutes on 2026-04-08, the auth service rejected ~8.4% of refresh
token requests as stale. No data loss; users were re-authenticated on
next active request.

## Root cause

Clock skew between auth nodes after the NTP daemon on one pod crashed
silently. Our staleness check used issued_at directly instead of
issued_at - SKEW_TOLERANCE (see PR #1423).

[Page 1]:
Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks

Patrick Lewis, Ethan Perez, Aleksandra Piktus, Fabio Petroni, Vladimir
Karpukhin, Naman Goyal, Heinrich Küttler, Mike Lewis, Wen-tau Yih,
Tim Rocktäschel, Sebastian Riedel, Douwe Kiela

Facebook AI Research / UCL / NYU

Abstract

Large pre-trained language models have been shown to store factual
knowledge in their parameters, and achieve state-of-the-art results
when fine-tuned on downstream NLP tasks. However, their ability to
access and precisely manipulate knowledge is still limited, and
hence on knowledge-intensive tasks, their performance lags behind
task-specific architectures.

[Page 1]:
The Story of the Little Hens

Once upon a time there were three little hens who lived at the
edge of the forest. Each day they ventured out to find crumbs and
seeds to carry back to their coop. The first hen was clever, the
second hen was patient, and the third hen was brave.

One morning a fox appeared at the treeline. The brave hen did not
run. She stood her ground and raised her wings. The fox, surprised
by her size, turned and slunk back into the trees.

7.1 Preflight checks

  - ECC is enabled and error counters are zero
  - Temperature < 55°C idle
  - Power draw matches expected baseline

[Page 1]:
Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models

Jared Kaplan, Sam McCandlish, Tom Henighan, Tom B. Brown, Benjamin Chess,
Rewon Child, Scott Gray, Alec Radford, Jeffrey Wu, Dario Amodei

OpenAI / Johns Hopkins

Abstract

We study empirical scaling laws for language model performance on the
cross-entropy loss. The loss scales as a power-law with model size,
dataset size, and the amount of compute used for training, with some
trends spanning more than seven orders of magnitude.

[Page 1]:
Safety evaluation of a constitutional-AI system prompt

Internal report. Draft.

Abstract

We evaluate the impact of a 400-token constitutional prompt on a
4B-parameter instruct model across four adversarial prompt suites:
prompt injection, jailbreak attempts, factual-fiction prompts, and
contextual harm prompts. The constitutional variant reduces unsafe
responses from 9.2% to 1.1% absolute, without measurable degradation
on benign task benchmarks.
